# Git for Advanced Users

Chapter 5 covered the daily loop on a single line of history: edit,
stage, commit, undo. That is most of the Git you need. This chapter
adds the tools for when one line is not enough: branches to develop a
robustness check without disturbing the main analysis, merging and
rebasing to bring that work back, and a set of power tools (stash,
bisect, cherry-pick, tags) that each solve one specific research
problem. The mental model from Chapter 3 still holds throughout: a
commit is a snapshot, and a branch is just a movable pointer at one of
those snapshots.

Every command block below was run against a throwaway clone of the
running example's `baseline-4commits` tag. The output is real, lightly
normalized only where it would otherwise mislead on the page:
carriage-return progress bars are collapsed to their final line, and
machine-specific paths are abbreviated. Your hashes will differ; the
behaviour will not.

## Branches and merging {.tier-beginner}

A branch lets you build something uncertain without risking the
version that works. You start a branch, commit freely on it, and the
`main` line stays exactly where it was. Creating and switching to one
is a single command.

```bash
$ git switch -c robust-se
Switched to a new branch 'robust-se'
```

Suppose on `robust-se` you add a note about clustering standard errors
to `report.qmd` and commit it. Meanwhile, back on `main`, you make an
unrelated edit (a cross-reference in the README) and commit that. The
two branches have now *diverged*: each has a commit the other does
not. Bringing them together is a merge.

```bash
$ git switch main
Switched to branch 'main'
$ git merge --no-edit robust-se
Merge made by the 'ort' strategy.
 report.qmd | 2 ++
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
```

Because the two branches changed different files, Git combined them
with no conflict and recorded a **merge commit**, a commit with two
parents that ties the histories back together.

```bash
$ git log --oneline --graph -5
*   3f60bca Merge branch 'robust-se'
|\
| * d938421 Note clustered-SE robustness check in report
* | 05c4464 Cross-reference the report from the README
|/
* dcb18de Note headline result in README
* f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
```

The graph shows the split and the rejoin: the two parallel commits,
and the merge commit `3f60bca` above them. This is the honest record
of how the work actually happened.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If you start a merge and decide you do not want it (wrong branch,
unexpected scope), `git merge --abort` returns you to the exact state
before you ran `git merge`. Nothing is committed until the merge
completes, so backing out is always safe.
:::

## When a merge conflicts {.tier-beginner}

Git merges different files automatically. It cannot merge two
*different edits to the same line*, because it has no way to know which
one you meant. That is a conflict, and in collaborative research it is
routine, not a crisis. Here a coauthor reworded the headline result on
one branch while you added a standard error to the same line on `main`.

```bash
$ git merge --no-edit coauthor-wording
Auto-merging README.md
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in README.md
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.
```

Git stops and tells you exactly where it is stuck. `git status`
confirms the state and, usefully, lists your options.

```bash
$ git status
On branch main
You have unmerged paths.
  (fix conflicts and run "git commit")
  (use "git merge --abort" to abort the merge)

Unmerged paths:
  (use "git add <file>..." to mark resolution)
	both modified:   README.md

no changes added to commit (use "git add" and/or "git commit -a")
```

Open the file and Git has marked the clash with conflict markers:
your version between `<<<<<<<` and `=======`, the incoming version
between `=======` and `>>>>>>>`.

```bash
$ sed -n '36,46p' README.md
## Headline result

<<<<<<< HEAD
The DiD estimate is a 1.9 pp drop in treated counties (SE 0.4 pp).
=======
Treated counties saw teen employment fall by about 1.9 percentage points.
>>>>>>> coauthor-wording
```

You resolve a conflict by editing the file into the single version you
want and deleting all three marker lines. Then you stage the resolved
file and commit, which completes the merge.

```bash
$ git add README.md
$ git commit --no-edit -q
$ git log --oneline --graph -4
*   067058c Merge branch 'coauthor-wording'
|\
| * 362b852 Reword headline result (coauthor)
* | 40a7427 Add SE to headline result
|/
* dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
A conflict is resolved by *what you leave in the file*, not by which
side you pick. It is entirely possible to delete the markers and still
leave a broken sentence, a duplicated line, or both edits stitched
together wrong. After resolving, re-read the actual text and, for code,
re-run it. Git checks that the markers are gone; it cannot check that
the result is correct.
:::

## Rebase: a tidier alternative to merge {.tier-advanced}

A merge preserves the true, branching shape of history. Sometimes you
would rather pretend the branching never happened and present a clean,
linear story instead. That is what `git rebase` does: it lifts the
commits from your branch and *replays* them, one by one, on top of the
latest `main`, as if you had started your work from there.

```bash
$ git rebase main
Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/add-event-study.
```

```bash
$ git log --oneline --graph -5
* 982d3de Sketch event-study figure in report
* cda9799 Document the employment-rate definition
* dcb18de Note headline result in README
* f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
* 758175e Add polars cleaning script
```

There is no merge commit and no fork in the graph: just a straight
line. Note that the rebased commit got a *new hash* (`982d3de`). Rebase
does not move commits, it rewrites them, because each commit's identity
depends on its parent and the parent changed.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The golden rule of rebasing: **never rebase commits you have already
pushed and shared.** Because rebase rewrites commits into new ones,
anyone who already has the old commits will find their history in
conflict with yours, and merging the two back together duplicates every
commit. Rebase your own local, unpushed work to tidy it before sharing;
once it is public, prefer merge. (Chapter 12 returns to this in the
context of pull requests.)
:::

## When a rebase goes wrong {.tier-advanced}

Rebasing is the most common way researchers scare themselves, usually
during an *interactive* rebase (`git rebase -i`) meant to tidy a messy
branch. Here a branch has three commits, and the middle one records a
real decision worth keeping.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -4
b511397 Tidy README whitespace
5d44844 Record clustering decision (KEEP THIS)
93e80ec Add placebo-test TODO
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

An interactive rebase opens an editor listing those commits, one per
line, for you to reorder, squash, or drop. If you accidentally delete
the wrong line, the corresponding commit is dropped silently and the
rebase reports plain success.

```bash
$ git rebase -i HEAD~3
Successfully rebased and updated refs/heads/cleanup-history.

$ git log --oneline -4
ea2e32a Tidy README whitespace
93e80ec Add placebo-test TODO
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
```

The "KEEP THIS" commit is gone from the branch, with no warning. This
is exactly the moment people panic. They should not, because Git wrote
down where the branch was before the rebase. The **reflog** is Git's
private log of every position `HEAD` has held.

```bash
$ git reflog -6
ea2e32a HEAD@{0}: rebase (finish): returning to refs/heads/cleanup-history
ea2e32a HEAD@{1}: rebase (pick): Tidy README whitespace
93e80ec HEAD@{2}: rebase (start): checkout HEAD~3
b511397 HEAD@{3}: commit: Tidy README whitespace
5d44844 HEAD@{4}: commit: Record clustering decision (KEEP THIS)
93e80ec HEAD@{5}: commit: Add placebo-test TODO
```

`HEAD@{3}` (`b511397`) is the branch tip from just before the rebase
started. Git also saves that tip under the name `ORIG_HEAD`, so you can
reset to it without copying a hash by hand.

```bash
$ git reset --hard ORIG_HEAD
HEAD is now at b511397 Tidy README whitespace
$ git log --oneline -4
b511397 Tidy README whitespace
5d44844 Record clustering decision (KEEP THIS)
93e80ec Add placebo-test TODO
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

All three commits are back, including the one that was dropped.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
After any rebase, reset, or merge you regret, `git reset --hard
ORIG_HEAD` returns to the state from immediately before it. For older
mistakes, run `git reflog`, find the `HEAD@{n}` you want, and reset to
that hash (or, more cautiously, `git switch -c rescue <hash>` to inspect
it on a new branch first). Reflog entries that are still reachable are
kept 90 days by default, but commits orphaned by a reset or rebase
become *unreachable* and expire in 30 (`gc.reflogExpireUnreachable`).
That is still weeks, not seconds, but recover sooner rather than later.
:::

## Stash: park work without committing {.tier-beginner}

Sometimes you are halfway through a messy experiment when something
urgent needs a clean working tree: a colleague's bug to reproduce, a
quick number for a meeting. You do not want to commit half-finished
work just to get it out of the way. `git stash` sets your uncommitted
changes aside and restores a clean tree.

```bash
$ git status --short
 M scripts/02_analysis.py
$ git stash
Saved working directory and index state WIP on main: dcb18de Note headline result in README
$ git status --short
$ git stash list
stash@{0}: WIP on main: dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

The working tree is clean and the change is safely held in the stash.
Do the urgent task, then bring the experiment back with `git stash
pop`, which reapplies the changes and removes them from the stash.

```bash
$ git stash pop
On branch main
Changes not staged for commit:
  (use "git add <file>..." to update what will be committed)
  (use "git restore <file>..." to discard changes in working directory)
	modified:   scripts/02_analysis.py

no changes added to commit (use "git add" and/or "git commit -a")
Dropped refs/stash@{0} (7c426ba52bbb8e58b3bd61ea4f8d9c47ca01d2eb)
```

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The stash is a convenience, not storage. It is local, easy to forget,
and a long stack of `stash@{3}`, `stash@{4}`, and so on is a sign that
work should have been a branch instead. Use it for
minutes-to-hours interruptions; for anything you might still want next
week, commit it on a branch.
:::

## Bisect: find the commit that broke a result {.tier-advanced}

This is the power tool researchers underuse the most. A number that was
right last month is now wrong, and somewhere in dozens of commits a
change broke it. `git bisect` finds the culprit by binary search:
you mark one known-good and one known-bad commit, and Git checks out
the midpoint repeatedly, halving the suspect range each time.

It is at its best with a script that returns success or failure. Here a
tiny smoke test exits 0 if the headline DiD coefficient is negative
(as it must be) and 1 if it is not. The bug: somewhere the sign of the
effect flipped.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -6
b0491dc Record county count
069243a Add notes pointer
8784fdd Refactor effect constants
151b299 Stub event-study coefficients
5fda8a3 Record policy month
067c7d0 Add effect module and a sign smoke test
```

Mark the bad and good ends, then hand the test to `git bisect run` and
let it converge on its own.

```bash
$ git bisect start
$ git bisect bad HEAD
$ git bisect good 067c7d0
Bisecting: 2 revisions left to test after this (roughly 1 step)
[151b2990159b57e956e4c632a23a7efee8f80824] Stub event-study coefficients
$ git bisect run python3 analysis/smoke_test.py
running  'python3' 'analysis/smoke_test.py'
OK: effect -0.0189 is negative
Bisecting: 0 revisions left to test after this (roughly 1 step)
[069243ae15525213ab2c561ce2118878e2c98fb5] Add notes pointer
running  'python3' 'analysis/smoke_test.py'
FAIL: effect 0.0189 is not negative
Bisecting: 0 revisions left to test after this (roughly 0 steps)
[8784fdd6bcc1320889674ea495f69ce91d8c54a8] Refactor effect constants
running  'python3' 'analysis/smoke_test.py'
FAIL: effect 0.0189 is not negative
8784fdd6bcc1320889674ea495f69ce91d8c54a8 is the first bad commit
commit 8784fdd6bcc1320889674ea495f69ce91d8c54a8
Author: Pat Lee <pat@example.edu>
Date:   Sat Feb 7 09:04:00 2026 +0000

    Refactor effect constants

 analysis/effect.py | 2 +-
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
bisect found first bad commit
```

Git names the exact commit that introduced the bug, `8784fdd`, after
testing only three. Always end with `git bisect reset` to return to
where you started.

```bash
$ git bisect reset
Previous HEAD position was 8784fdd Refactor effect constants
Switched to branch 'main'
```

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Bisect only works if each old commit can actually be rebuilt and
tested. This is the payoff of the habits in this book: a seeded data
generator (Chapter 8), a pipeline that runs from code, and small
frequent commits mean any past commit is a working checkpoint a script
can interrogate. A repo of giant manual commits with un-rebuildable
data cannot be bisected. Reproducibility is not only for other people;
it is what lets *you* debug your own history.
:::

## Cherry-pick: take one commit, not the branch {.tier-advanced}

Occasionally you want a single commit from another branch without
merging everything on it. A `hotfix` branch has a finished typo fix and
an unfinished experiment sitting on top of it.

```bash
$ git log --oneline -3
cea9aa3 WIP bootstrap SEs (not ready)
c525fb3 Fix typo in README
dcb18de Note headline result in README
```

You want the typo fix on `main` now, but not the work in progress.
`git cherry-pick` copies just that one commit across.

```bash
$ git cherry-pick c525fb3
[main 388cff5] Fix typo in README
 Date: Fri Feb 6 08:01:00 2026 +0000
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
$ git log --oneline -3
388cff5 Fix typo in README
dcb18de Note headline result in README
f9a193f Add DiD analysis, report, and main table
```

The fix is now on `main` as commit `388cff5`, a *new* hash: like
rebase, cherry-pick rewrites the commit onto a new parent rather than
moving it.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Because the cherry-picked commit is a copy, the same change now exists
under two different hashes on two branches. If you later merge those
branches, Git is usually smart enough to notice, but cherry-picking
heavily and then merging can resurrect changes or cause confusing
conflicts. Reach for cherry-pick for the occasional targeted fix, not
as a routine way to move work around.
:::

## Tags: name a version you can return to {.tier-beginner}

A commit hash like `dcb18de` is precise but meaningless to a human. A
**tag** attaches a permanent, readable name to a specific commit. The
kind you want is an *annotated* tag, which stores its own message,
author, and date.

```bash
$ git tag -a v1.0-wp -m 'Working paper submission, Feb 2026'
$ git tag
baseline-4commits
v1.0-wp
$ git show --no-patch v1.0-wp
tag v1.0-wp
Tagger: Pat Lee <pat@example.edu>
Date:   Fri Feb 6 08:30:00 2026 +0000

Working paper submission, Feb 2026

commit dcb18de650c577b036ffc179a1b4c6544a2c838b
Author: Pat Researcher <pat@example.edu>
Date:   Thu Jun 4 11:00:00 2026 +0000

    Note headline result in README
```

The tag `v1.0-wp` now permanently marks the exact code behind that
submission. Unlike a branch, a tag does not move as you keep working,
which is exactly why it is the right tool for "this is what the
referees saw."

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Tags are the backbone of a citable replication package. When you submit
a paper, tag the commit; when you post the package to a repository that
mints a DOI (Chapter 15), you push the tag so the archived version and
the Git history point at the same snapshot. "Results in Table 3 use
release `v1.0-wp`" is reproducible in a way that "the latest code" never
is.
:::

## What you can now do

You can run parallel lines of work with branches and bring them back
with `merge`, resolve the conflicts that arise when two edits collide,
and choose between a truthful merge history and a tidy rebased one,
knowing the golden rule that keeps rebase safe. You can park work with
`stash`, hunt a regression to its exact commit with `bisect`, lift a
single commit with `cherry-pick`, and name a version with an annotated
`tag`. And underneath all of it, the reflog means almost nothing you do
locally is truly unrecoverable. Chapter 9 takes this history online.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Work in a throwaway clone of the running example so you cannot harm real
history (`git clone <bundle> practice && cd practice`).

1. Create a branch `robustness`, add a commit that appends a robustness
   note to `report.qmd`, switch back to `main`, add a different commit,
   then `git merge` the branch. Confirm with `git log --oneline --graph`
   that you got a merge commit with two parents.
2. Force a conflict on purpose: edit the same README line on two
   branches and merge them. Resolve it, then re-read the resulting line
   to check it is a real sentence, not stitched-together fragments.
3. On a fresh branch with three small commits, run `git rebase -i
   HEAD~3` and drop the middle commit. Recover it with `git reset --hard
   ORIG_HEAD`, and explain why the reflog still had it.
4. Break a number on purpose (flip a sign in a small constant), bury it
   under a couple of commits, write a one-line test that exits nonzero
   when the number is wrong, and let `git bisect run` find the commit.
   This is the single most useful exercise in the chapter.
5. Tag your starting commit `v0.1-practice` with an annotated tag, then
   confirm with `git show v0.1-practice` that the message is stored in
   the tag itself.
